title: Application Processing
html:
Application Processing

Application Processing

A Uniface application may be started by running a application shell or activating a component. What happens next depends on the type of component, the user actions and system events, and the scripted instructions for responding to those events.

When Uniface receives a request for a component, it creates an instance of the specified component using the definitions of the runtime object, and executes the default or requested script module.

Uniface has its own scripting language, called ProcScript, which is used to program application behavior. ProcScript encapsulates complex processing (such as SQL and transaction handling) into single instructions, making it possible to program complex behaviors quickly and easily. Most script instructions are ProcScript, but browser-side behavior can be scripted using JavaScript.

Script modules are named collection of script belonging to a development object.

  • Triggers are object-specific script modules that define the application behavior in response to user or system event.

    Interactive triggers are executed in response to user actions (the user clicks a button or moves a cursor).

    System triggers are executed in response to system events (the validation trigger of an occurrence is executed only when the validation trigger of a modified field has successfully completed).

  • Operations and functions are executed only when they are explicitly invoked by a Proc instruction or external component. They are analogous to methods in other programming languages.

Web and Mobile Apps

Web and mobile apps are stateless, and rely on a native browser to interpret events and communicate between the client browser and the server. Uniface generates HTML to be displayed in the browser. The first time a page is loaded, it also includes a JavaScript-based runtime engine to control Uniface application behavior in the browser.

Some triggers and operations can be implemented as JavaScript and executed solely in the browser, while ProcScript is used for server-side triggers and operations.

Desktop Applications

For desktop applications, the mechanism Uniface uses to receive and interpret events, and ensure that all processing occurs correctly, is the structure editor. The structure editor is the event handler for form components. It manages the external data structure that exists when a component contains data.

For example, if a user changes data in a field, this change is made in the external data structure (which resides in memory). The structure editor implicitly executes some triggers to perform data validation or syntax checking.

User interface controls, such as keystrokes, function keys, and menu items are mapped to structure editor functions, which activate specific triggers. Structure editor functions can also be executed by Proc code.

Related Links
  • Structure Editor

    The structure editor is the mechanism in Uniface that understands events in form components. The structure editor derives its name from the external data structure that exists when forms contain data.

  • Trigger Activation

    Triggers can be activated by events originating from user actions, ProcScript statements, and external events (such as asynchronous interrupts).

  • ProcScript Syntax

    ProcScript provides high-level statements, functions, expressions, and operators that enable you to efficiently manipulate data and script application behavior. It is a powerful language that condenses complex processing into single commands.

  • Script Modules

    A script module is a named collection of ProcScript belonging to a development object. Script modules are analogous to events and methods in other programming languages.
